source_metadata.title_raw: Keith Storton - A-19-011 - February 19, 2019 - Arroyo Grande

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Keith Storton 
City Councilmember 
300 E. Branch Street 
Arroyo Grande, CA 93420 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-011 
Dear Mr. Storton: 
February 19, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding conflict of interest provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
As an Arroyo Grande City Councilmember, can you take part in governmental decisions 
relating to the· Strawberry Festival in Arroyo Grande, given that you have approximately $5,000 
invested in a business that may be financially affected by those decisions? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes, you may take part in the decision under the Act's public generally exception. Based on 
the facts provided, it appears that the Strawberry Festival will likely affect sales for at least 25 
percent of Arroyo Grande businesses and there is no indication of a unique effect on your business. 
Accordingly, the decisions' financial effect on your interest is indistinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-19-011 
Page No. 2 
You have recently been elected a City Councilmember of Arroyo Grande, a city that is 
approximately six square miles, with approximately 18,000 residents. You and your wife own 100 
shares of an ice cream parlor business named Doc Burnstein's, valued at approximately $5,000. The 
company has sold approximately 28,000 shares to some 400 investors, and in your several years of 
ownership, no profits have been distributed to investors. 
The original Doc Burnstein's is located in Arroyo Grande, but other locations have-opened 
in surrounding communities. The original Doc Burnstein's location is in the "downtown Village 
core of Arroyo Grande." The popular shop is open every day and busy most days. The downtown 
Village core covers approximately six city blocks and contains approximately 100 shops, including 
a variety of retail stores and restaurants. Doc Burnstein's is the only ice cream shop in the area. 
The private Village Improvement Association sponsors a local event called the "Strawberry 
Festival." Approximately 100,000 people attend over a two-day period, and approximately 300 
vendors attend, with about 70 of them serving food. Within those 70, some vendors serve treats and 
strawberry-type desserts. The City provides funding, services, and personnel to help off-set 
expenses. To conduct the Strawberry Festival, the "Village" is closed to through-traffic and the 
space is used for festival booths, bands, and events, among other things. The Arroyo Grande Doc 
Burnstein' s is located in that closed-off area and remains busy during the Festival. 
The Arroyo Grande City Council may be faced with the decision whether to support the 
festival and keep the roadway closed or no longer provide funds or services to support the festival. 
If the City of Arroyo Grande does not provide support, there is concern that the festival may cease 
to exist, or would operate in a different format. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, 
or in any way attempting to use her official position to influence a governmental decision in which 
she knows or has reason to know she has a financial interest. 
Interests 
Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a decision, within the 
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on one or more of the official's interests. Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise 
are defined in Section 87103, and include any business entity in which the public official has a 
direct or indirect investment worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
Here, you have acknowledged that you have a direct investment in Doc Burnstein's, a business, 
worth more than $2,000. Therefore, you have a business entity interest in Doc Burnstein's. 


Foreseeability and Materiality 
FileNo. A-19-011 
Page No. 3 
A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the 
financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official 
or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) Regarding financial interests not explicitly involved 
in a decision, a financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. "In 
general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical 
or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent 
extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably 
foreseeable." (Regulation 18701(b).) 
Generally, where a governmental decision will have a financial effect on a business entity 
not explicitly involved in that decision, the materiality of the financial effect is analyzed under 
Regulation 18702.1 (b ). That regulation states: "the financial effect is material if a prudent person 
with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial 
effect would contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the 
value of a privately-held business entity." Examples of potentially applicable decisions include 
those that: 
• 
Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business entity supplies; 
• 
Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as redevelopment projects, 
traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may affect, either temporarily or 
permanently, the amount of business the business entity receives; or 
• 
Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or other project that 
would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity draws from the location of the 
project. 
(Regulation 18702.l(b)(3)-(5).) 
Here, Doc Bumstein's is not expressly involved in the City Council's decision on whether to 
support the Strawberry Festival. Thus, the reasonable foreseeability of a financial effect on your 
business entity interest is determined by considering whether a financial effect can be recognized as 
a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b ).) The Festival 
brings in 100,000 people to the vicinity (in other words, potential customers) over a two-day period. 
Doc Bumstein's could realistically see an increase profitability based on the sheer volume ·of 
customers that weekend. Accordingly, a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable. For the same 
reason-that is, a significant increase in the amount of business that Doc Bumstein's would likely 
receive because of the festival-the financial effect would also be material. 
However, the Act only prohibits a public official from partaking in a decision if the 
decision's effect on the official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
(Section 87103.) Commonly referred to as the "public generally exception," Regulation 18703(a) 
sets forth the general rule: 
A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial interest is 
indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official establishes that a 


FileNo. A-19-011 
Page No. 4 
significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her financial 
interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant segment. 
For purposes of the exception, a "significant segment of the public" consists of at least 25 percent 
of (1) the businesses or nonprofit entities; (2) the real property, commercial or residential; or (3) the 
individuals within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b).) 
Arroyo Grande is a small city, approximately six square miles in size, with approximately 
18,000 residents. The Strawberry Festival, in contrast, is attended by approximately 100,000 people 
and 300 additional vendors, and the area covers Arroyo Grande's "downtown Village core," 
including approximately 6 blocks and 100 stores. Additionally, the impact of the festival, in terms 
of bringing additional customers to Arroyo Grande businesses, would likely exceed well beyond the 
"downtown Village core" area. In other words, it appears that at least 25 percent of Arroyo Grande 
businesses would likely see a comparable increase in profitability based on an increased number of 
customers brought in by the Strawberry Festival. Moreover, there is no indication that the 
Strawberry Festival might have a unique effect on Doc Bumstein's as compared to other businesses 
affected by the decision. 
Accordingly, under the Act, your interest in Doc Bumstein's does not prohibit you from 
taking part in governmental decisions pertaining to support of the Strawberry Festival. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
KMC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
8L-.____// 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division
